text.title: Exploratory Charter Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every exploratory testing session must satisfy. The discipline borrows directly from Session-Based Test Management; these rules prevent the session from drifting into unstructured poking.

rule.statement: The charter has ONE mission, written down before the session starts. Mission format: "Explore [target] to discover [class of information]." If the explorer cannot articulate a mission in one sentence, the session is not ready.

rationale.source: Bach and Bolton SBTM, 2003; cognitive-load research on goal-directed exploration

rule.rationale: Unstructured exploration becomes ambient bug hunting; the explorer ends up at random pages without a thread to follow. A single mission focuses attention; multi-mission sessions split it. The pre-commit is the difference between a session and a "let me have a poke around".

example.title: Charter with single mission
example.code:
# Charter — 2026-05-19, exploration session #14
Mission: Explore the new email-onboarding flow to discover failure modes in internationalisation, timezone handling, and email-rendering across providers.
Target: /onboarding/email/* (3 steps) Time-box: 75 minutes (15 min prep, 50 min explore, 10 min debrief) Explorer: Ruslan (no input on the implementation; first-time user view)

rule.statement: Sessions are time-boxed: 60, 75, 90, or 120 minutes maximum. The timer starts at the first observation log entry and stops when the time-box expires. Extending mid-session is forbidden; if the mission needs more time, schedule a follow-up session with its own charter.
rule.rationale: Beyond 90 minutes, exploratory testing degrades sharply — same dimensions, same blind spots, same bugs re-found. A strict time-box also prevents the session from absorbing the rest of the day. Follow-up sessions are explicitly named follow-ups, not silent extensions.

rule.statement: The explorer logs every test idea attempted, every observation, every question, every bug — with a timestamp. Logs are written DURING the session, not reconstructed after. The log is the audit trail; missing-during-session entries are excluded from the debrief.

rationale.source: qualitative-research field-notes practice; SBTM log discipline

rule.rationale: Reconstructed logs lose the load-bearing detail (exact input that triggered the bug, exact order of operations, micro-symptoms). Continuous logging is the only mechanism that preserves the information the debrief needs.

example.title: Observation log entry
example.code: 14:23 Idea: try unicode emoji in onboarding name field (heuristic: input variety) 14:24 Tried 👨‍👩‍👧 — accepted, but step 2 displays raw bytes \xf0\x9f... 14:26 Filed bug #2118: ZWJ emoji not rendered in welcome email 14:27 Question: does this affect support tickets too? → follow-up 14:30 Idea: same input via API → bypasses UI sanitiser — log

rule.statement: Within 24 hours of the session, the explorer publishes a debrief: 3-5 key findings, list of bugs filed with severity, follow-up questions, debt items (test gaps the session revealed). The debrief is posted to the team channel and indexed in the QA wiki.
rule.rationale: Debriefs delayed beyond 24h lose nuance and become "the bug list" without the broader patterns the session revealed (e.g. "all i18n bugs concentrate in step 2"). Patterns are the load-bearing output of exploratory testing; the bugs themselves are by-products.

rule.statement: Exploratory sessions DO NOT execute pre-written test cases. The charter lists 3-5 focus areas and applicable heuristics; the explorer generates test ideas in the moment. A session that follows a checklist of test cases is regression testing, not exploration, and should be moved to the automated suite.

rationale.source: Bach core SBTM principle; exploratory vs scripted distinction

rule.rationale: Pre-written cases constrain the explorer's attention to what has already been thought of; the entire value of exploration is generating ideas in response to what the system reveals. Mixing the two modes dilutes both.

rule.statement: Stop applying this methodology when the product has no user-meaningful surface, the build is broken, the team has no exploratory capacity, OR the only available explorer is biased (wrote the code) without a second perspective — in those cases the session produces noise instead of signal.
rule.rationale: Exploratory testing on a broken build or by a biased explorer wastes the time-box. The skip leaf in the decision tree resolves to this rule when any precondition fails.

list.item: SFDPOT (Structure, Function, Data, Platform, Operations, Time) — Bach generic coverage
CRUD per resource — Create, Read, Update, Delete; common gap is Update edge cases
Input variety — empty, max-length, unicode, control chars, leading/trailing whitespace
Timing — fast clicks, slow clicks, concurrent actions, refresh mid-action
State transitions — what if the user navigates back, what if they leave and return
Permissions — what if the user role changes mid-session
Network — slow, offline, packet loss, request retries
